title: Chassis PSU Redundancy Loss (Cisco UCS)
exclusions: Cisco Intersight–managed (or Intersight-primary) UCS domains where you intentionally consume chassis power, redundancy, and power-domain posture through the Intersight cloud, connector, or on-prem appliance stream rather than a classic UCS Manager XML poll into the Splunk add-on; standalone C-series rack servers in CIMC mode that never register a `sys/chassis-…` anchor in the UCSM inventory you index (they have a different object tree, so a chassis-PSU posture query would be misleading); sites that only forward UCS through UCS Director or a legacy re-shaper that re-labels or drops `classId` on inventory and stats events; fully rack-and-stack estates with no 5108-style blade chassis; and any domain where the statistics modular input is not licensed or enabled, leaving `cisco:ucs:stats` empty for `equipmentChassisStats` while you still have partial inventory, which would make this UC appear worse than the facility reality. Those scenarios need the alternative product, not silent alert tuning here.
dataSources: Primary index `cisco_ucs` (or the index your add-on is configured to write) with sourcetype `cisco:ucs:inventory` (XML inventory for `equipmentPsu` rows with per-slot `operState` and for `equipmentChassis` model and administrative power attributes) AND sourcetype `cisco:ucs:stats` (poll statistics where `classId="equipmentChassisStats"` exposes the live `psuRedundancy` enumeration). A fictional sourcetype `cisco:ucs:environmental` does not exist in the Splunk add-on; treat any runbook that cites it as a documentation error and use `cisco:ucs:stats` with a `classId` filter, per Splunkbase 2731. Managed object classes: `equipmentPsu` (per-PSU operability on the DNs under each chassis), `equipmentChassis` (anchor DNs and model under `sys/chassis-…` for baselines and policy hints), and `equipmentChassisStats` (chassis-level computed redundancy).
app: Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS, app directory name `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` (Splunkbase 2731; the on-disk and publish id use an underscore; v4.3.x is typical on Splunk 9.x/10.x and Splunk Cloud and matches the `classId`+`sourcetype` model used here; package filenames may show a hyphen from packaging, which is not a different add-on). Install on a Heavy Forwarder (or the tier that runs the UCS modular inputs) and on Search Heads for saved searches, field discovery, and time-bound lookups. Verify both inventory and statistics inputs are enabled for the same UCSM endpoint before you build redundancy posture; half a feed produces half a story.
spl:
index=cisco_ucs (sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" OR sourcetype="cisco:ucs:stats") earliest=-30m@m
/* UC-19.1.15 — Chassis PSU redundancy posture (inventory PSUs + chassis stats; NOT the UC-19.1.6 environmental threshold board) */
| eval cidl=lower(coalesce(classId, class_id, ""))
| where (sourcetype="cisco:ucs:stats" AND cidl="equipmentchassisstats") OR (sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" AND (cidl="equipmentpsu" OR cidl="equipmentchassis"))
| rex field=dn max_match=0 "^(?<chassis_dn>sys/chassis-[^/]+)(/|$)"
| where isnotnull(chassis_dn) AND match(chassis_dn, "^sys/chassis-")
| eval oper=lower(coalesce(operState, oper_state, operstate, ""))
| eval is_oper=if(cidl="equipmentpsu" AND (oper=="operable" OR oper=="operational" OR oper=="ok" OR oper=="online"),1,0)
| eval is_psu_row=if(cidl="equipmentpsu",1,0)
| eval chassis_model_t=if(cidl="equipmentchassis", coalesce(model, mdl, modelStr, productName, product_name, ""), null())
| eval pru=if(cidl="equipmentchassisstats", lower(trim(coalesce(psuRedundancy, psu_redundancy, ""))), null())
| eval ag_hint=if(cidl="equipmentchassis", lower(trim(toString(coalesce(powerCapping, adminPowerCappingPolicy, adminPowerPolicy, powerPolicy, "")))), null())
| sort 0 chassis_dn, -_time, cidl
| stats sum(is_oper) as psu_operational_count, sum(is_psu_row) as psu_total_count, latest(chassis_model_t) as chassis_model, latest(pru) as current_redundancy_state, latest(ag_hint) as admin_from_mo by chassis_dn
| lookup ucs_chassis_psu_baseline chassis_model OUTPUT default_admin_goal as lookup_goal, psu_expected, notes as baseline_notes
| fillnull value="" lookup_goal, baseline_notes, admin_from_mo, chassis_model, current_redundancy_state
| eval admin_redundancy_goal=if(lookup_goal!="", lookup_goal, if(len(admin_from_mo)>2, admin_from_mo, "n+1"))
| eval current_redundancy_state=if(current_redundancy_state=="", "unknown", current_redundancy_state)
| eval redundancy_delta=case(
    (chassis_model=="" OR isnull(chassis_model)), "unknown_admin_goal",
    psu_operational_count=0, "imminent_power_loss",
    psu_operational_count=1 AND psu_total_count>1, "degraded_one_psu_loss_from_outage",
    (match(current_redundancy_state, "(non-?redundant|not-?redundant|nonredundant)")) OR (current_redundancy_state="non-redundant"), "at_capacity_no_redundancy",
    (current_redundancy_state="redundant" OR current_redundancy_state="grid-redundant" OR current_redundancy_state="n+1") AND (psu_operational_count=psu_total_count) AND (psu_total_count>0), "intact",
    1=1, "at_capacity_no_redundancy"
  )
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    redundancy_delta=="imminent_power_loss", "critical",
    redundancy_delta=="degraded_one_psu_loss_from_outage" OR redundancy_delta=="at_capacity_no_redundancy" OR redundancy_delta=="unknown_admin_goal", "warning",
    redundancy_delta=="intact", "ok",
    1=1, "warning"
  )
| table chassis_dn, chassis_model, psu_total_count, psu_operational_count, admin_redundancy_goal, current_redundancy_state, redundancy_delta, severity_bucket, baseline_notes
| sort 0 severity_bucket, chassis_dn
description: Distinct from UC-19.1.6 (which trends environmental telemetry against per-class thresholds for kW, thermal, and fans), 19.1.15 joins a 30m slice of `cisco:ucs:inventory` (`equipmentPsu`, `equipmentChassis`) to `cisco:ucs:stats` `equipmentChassisStats` per `sys/chassis-…` `dn`, uses `coalesce()` for XML field variants, applies `lookup ucs_chassis_psu_baseline` for the admin goal, and outputs `redundancy_delta` and `severity_bucket` for discrete redundancy-posture loss, not 24h drift charts.
value: UC-19.1.6 answers whether cooling and power readings are still inside the bands you defined for a programme dashboard; this one answers whether the domain still has the N+1 or grid power posture the facility and capacity teams think they bought. When `equipmentPsu.operState` is no longer fully operable but workloads still run, a tenant sees nothing in the guest OS, yet you may be one more AC fault or RMA from a full chassis de-energize. In regulated and dual-feed sites, a drop from `grid-redundant` to a barely sustainable row after one PDU or grid side is taken down is a board-level operational story, not a hypervisor log story. A single joined table, refreshed every few minutes, gives the NOC a chassis-native reason to call data-center hands with the UCSM `dn` before finance reads about it as unplanned power minutes; pair it with 19.1.6 for design margin, with this for posture loss.
implementation: Enable both inventory and statistics inputs in `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` to the same UCSM. Add `lookups/ucs_chassis_psu_baseline.csv` and `transforms.conf` for `ucs_chassis_psu_baseline` on the SH. After `fieldsummary` proves `classId` fields, save the `spl` as a 5m alert: fire when `redundancy_delta` is not `intact` and `severity_bucket` is warning or critical, throttle on `chassis_dn`, and require two consecutive bad bins to absorb one inventory/stats offset.
visualization: Chassis × PSU `operState` heat grid from inventory. The SPL result table, filtered to non-`intact`, with UCSM link. Single value: chassis count in warning or critical. Optional 24h `timechart` of operable PSU count per `chassis_dn` for swap/RMA. Co-locate a row on the same tab as the UC-19.1.6 kW/thermal board so margin (19.1.6) and redundancy posture (19.1.15) are visible together.

references.title: Cisco UCS Manager Server Management / chassis power, capping, and redundancy context (GUI line — pair with 4.2 release notes in your org)
Cisco UCS Manager XML API Reference 4.2 — equipmentPsu, equipmentChassis, equipmentChassisStats, equipmentPsuStats managed objects
Splunk documentation — coalesce, case, and eval (field-normalisation and posture bucketing patterns)

knownFalsePositives:
• PSU RMA with an intentional 5–30 minute `removed` or `inoperable` per-slot state while a spare is in transit: page after dwell time, not the first single poll, and add a PagerDuty snooze for the ticketed FRU id.
• Intentional single-feed or single-side work on a dual-PDU or grid A/B test where the chassis legitimately downgrades from `grid-redundant` to `n+1` for an hour: align with the electrical change calendar and a suppress token by `chassis_dn` when the BMS and UCSM both expect the work.
• Older 5108 chassis that mix `UCSB-PSU-2500ACDV` and `UCSB-PSU-2500ACDV2` in one chassis after a field swap: the live `psuRedundancy` read can flicker for one to two poll intervals until both PSUs re-handshake and watt sharing stabilises; require two identical failing bins before a Sev-1 for flicker only.
• Standalone C-series images without a `sys/chassis-…` DNs or mis-scoped to the wrong org: if your index is broad, a rack-only `equipmentPsu` can appear; exclude by a tag that proves UCSM service-profile registration or drop events without `sys/chassis-` in the `dn` path.
• UCSM maintenance where `operState` briefly shows `accessibility-problem` for a single stats cycle during fabric or IOM reseat while no FRU is actually removed: correlate with a parallel `cisco:ucs:faults` `faultInst` and require two consecutive bad bins in this UC.
• Firmware waves that reboot one PSU at a time for vendor soak rules: a planned momentary `inoperable` is expected; gate with the firmware change record and a windowed suppression, not a permanent threshold cut.
• UCS X9508 and later X-chassis with different PSU counts, IFM, and 6+ PSU layouts than 5108: the default lookup row may label `n+1` while your contract is actually `n+n` full doubling; if so, annotate per-`chassis_model` in the lookup so `redundancy_delta` does not over-page when the site’s practice is to accept one off-line PSU without paging.
• Clock or poll skew that lands inventory a cycle ahead of `equipmentChassisStats`: a false gap between `psu_operational_count` and the live `psuRedundancy` string; implement the two-poll hold and compare `_time` spread before RMA on transient mismatches only.
• Laboratory chassis left intentionally on a single feed for current-limited hall testing: mark those DNs in a `suppress_chassis` lookup so operations does not get weekend noise unrelated to production posture.
• Sites that have already decommissioned a blade chassis but the UCSM org still has stale `equipmentChassis` rows after incomplete cleanup: zero PSUs in inventory with a phantom chassis row can look worse than real life; CMDB decommission the MO before you tune severity.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk add-on: `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` (Splunkbase 2731) on a Heavy Forwarder (or the allowed collection tier) and on Search Heads. A read-only UCSM service account is required; split inventory and statistics to the same index so `chassis_dn` lines up across sourcetypes. **Never** trust a `cisco:ucs:environmental` sourcetype: internal wikis may say it, but the shipped add-on does not publish it; the feeds you use here are `cisco:ucs:inventory` and `cisco:ucs:stats` with `classId` filters. UC-19.1.6 is the **monitoring and thermal programme**: it time-slices 24h of environmental statistics per MO class, compares to a per-metric threshold CSV, and classifies into OK, Warn, Critical, and NoBaseline for kW, inlet, fan RPM, and related counters. UC-19.1.15 is the **redundancy posture and paging** use case: it asks whether, given your administrative intent (N+1, grid, or site lookup baseline), the actual count of `equipmentPsu.operState` values that are still operable still satisfies that story on each `sys/chassis-…` anchor, and whether `equipmentChassisStats.psuRedundancy` already says the live UCSM evaluation is `non-redundant` or not. The MO classes: `equipmentPsu` in inventory (per-PSU operability, slot path under the chassis), `equipmentChassis` in inventory (model, sometimes administrative power or policy hints the TA may flatten with alternate spellings), and `equipmentChassisStats` in statistics (live `psuRedundancy` enum: values such as `redundant`, `n+1`, `non-redundant`, `grid-redundant` in your firmware; normalise in SPL with `lower()` and `coalesce()`). You need **both** inventory and stats because inventory tells you the inventory truth of which PSUs are physically there and their `operState`, while the chassis stats class carries UCSM’s aggregate redundancy label that is computed across those PSUs and the power policy; a gap between the two is exactly what on-call should investigate. **RBAC and TLS** mirror other UCS UCs: the polling user is not your browser admin; 443 to UCSM with a full chain in `cacerts` on the forwarder. The **admin-goal vs current-state** pattern: administrative intent may come from the baseline CSV (`default_admin_goal`) with optional MO hints, while current state is the live `psuRedundancy` string plus a hard count of operable supplies — when those disagree after two polls, treat it as a real incident, not a single-blip dashboard artefact.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection (inputs.conf) and the posture baseline lookup**
(1) In Splunk Web, ensure two modular input families are on: the Cisco UCS **inventory** input (sourcetype `cisco:ucs:inventory`, classes including `equipmentPsu` and `equipmentChassis`) and the **statistics** input (sourcetype `cisco:ucs:stats`, including `classId=equipmentChassisStats` at minimum; you may add `equipmentPsuStats` for a cross-check, but 19.1.15’s primary `spl` does not require it). (2) File-driven example on the collection host: create or edit `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs/local/inputs.conf` (never paste secrets in chat) with stanzas that point both streams at the same `https://<ucsm-fqdn>`, the same `index = cisco_ucs`, the vendor default sourcetype per stream, and `disabled=0` only after the password object exists in Splunk. A representative **literal** pair of stanza **shapes** (names must follow what the add-on you installed actually ships) looks like the following; copy from `default/inputs.conf` and adjust keys only as the vendor’s comments allow:

```ini
# === Inventory probe (illustrative keys — verify against Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs v4.3.x) ===
# [cisco_ucs://prod_ucs_inventory]
# connection = <reuse vendor stanza name>
# index = cisco_ucs
# mode = <inventory|config – follow vendor string>
# disabled = 0

# === Statistics probe ===
# [cisco_ucs://prod_ucs_stats]
# index = cisco_ucs
# class_list = <include equipmentChassisStats at minimum; expand per Cisco guidance>
# disabled = 0
```

(3) After two full poll periods, run discovery: `index=cisco_ucs earliest=-1h | stats count by sourcetype, classId` and confirm you see `cisco:ucs:inventory` with `classId=equipmentPsu` and `equipmentChassis`, and `cisco:ucs:stats` with `classId=equipmentChassisStats`.
(4) **Field-variant `coalesce()` strategy**: the TA can expose `operState`, `oper_state`, or mixed XML flattening; the SPL in the `spl` field already wraps common variants. If your `fieldsummary` for `equipmentPsu` shows only `oper_state`, add that string to the `coalesce()` in a local version of the saved search, not a second copy of the use case. (5) **Why `equipmentChassisStats.psuRedundancy` can lag `equipmentPsu.operState` by one to two polls**: the statistics poller and inventory poller have different cycle times and object fan-out; a PSU that just tripped to `inoperable` can appear in inventory before the chassis-level aggregate in stats catches up, or the reverse. The SPL in this use case does not average across days — it is a 30m window and `sort … -_time` with `latest()` so you always pick the most recent state per `chassis_dn`; operationally, add a 5–10 minute `dedup` or a two-failure required trigger on the alert to absorb single-bin skew. (6) On the Search Head, create the governance CSV and the transform as you did for UC-19.1.6’s environment thresholds, but for chassis posture baselines — path: `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/<your_sh_app>/lookups/ucs_chassis_psu_baseline.csv` and `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/<your_sh_app>/local/transforms.conf` for `[ucs_chassis_psu_baseline] filename = ucs_chassis_psu_baseline.csv` with the same `case_sensitive_match` and `min_matches` policy you use elsewhere.

**Step 2 — Create the search, the lookup, and the 5–15 minute page**
(1) Paste the `spl` value from this use case. (2) **Stage walkthrough, top to bottom**: the opening `OR` of sourcetypes limits cost; `cidl` is a case-normalised `classId` so you are not bitten by mixed-case XML class names. The `rex` on `dn` is the `sys/chassis-N` hand-off that prevents accidental joins to IOM, FI, or rack-unit paths; every blade chassis discussion in 19.1.15 is anchored to that string. The `is_oper` sum is a **count of PSUs in an operable family**; extend the `IN` list only if your XML enum uses an extra value your site proves is “good.” The `latest()` pull for `equipmentChassis` model and for stats `psuRedundancy` is time-ordered. The `lookup` enriches an administrative goal; if your CMDB is cleaner than the MO hint, the CSV should win. (3) **The `redundancy_delta` `case()`** maps `(lookup_goal, current_redundancy_state, psu_operational_count, psu_total_count)` to the five public strings. `intact` is only emitted when the live state string looks like a healthy redundancy class **and** the operable count matches the inventory-backed total, or the stats say redundant with room to spare. `imminent_power_loss` is unambiguous: zero operable. `degraded_one_psu_loss_from_outage` is the classic “only one good PSU left in a two-or-more-bay design.” `at_capacity_no_redundancy` is when UCSM already classifies the chassis as not redundant or your total vs operable count implies no more margin. `unknown_admin_goal` is a model string the lookup has no row for, so you can page low-severity to inventory, not the data centre floor. (4) **Literal** `lookups/ucs_chassis_psu_baseline.csv` (copy into the app and re-tune to your org):

```csv
chassis_model,default_admin_goal,psu_expected,notes
UCSB-5108-AC2,n+1,4,5108 AC; four-bay; common enterprise blade chassis
UCSB-5108-DC,grid-redundant,4,5108 DC feed design; some sites run grid on dual-DC A/B
UCSB-5108-HV,n+1,4,high-voltage PSU pack; use same count rules, verify with nameplate kW at install
UCSB-200M4,n+1,2,smaller 2-PSU form factor; loss of one is immediate posture loss
UCSB-200M5,n+1,2,2-PSU B200 in select integrations; not every site uses this OCP mezz family
UCSX-9508,n+1,6,X9508; six PSU; IFM and power policy differ from 5108 — do not copy kW from 19.1.6 panels blindly
UCS-5108-AC2, n+1,4,Alias row if your model string omits the leading UCSB- prefix the TA emits
S3260,n+1,2,Storage chassis with 2-PSU; verify against physical audit before you trust a template row
UCS-5108, n+1,4,Generic alias when the MO model string is shortened by firmware family
N20-C6508, n+1,4,Legacy-class reference row for older 5108 family strings still in the estate
CVR-CSC-2U, n+1,2,2U carrier style with paired PSUs; pair with a physical inventory if yours still exists
UCS-5108, n+1,4,Duplicate alias token for orgs with mixed nameplate strings; edit out once CMDB is clean
```

(5) The **lookup-driven** pattern matches `chassis_model` as emitted by the MO; you may need a second `lookup` for regex patterns if your string includes serial suffixes. (6) **Alerting**: `| where redundancy_delta!="intact"` and optionally down-tier `unknown_admin_goal` to email-only until the CSV is right.

**Step 3 — Validate against Cisco UCS Manager and the facility**
(1) Take one `chassis_dn` from the result table, paste it into the UCSM Equipment tree, and open the chassis Power or PSU view; compare the number of `operable` vs total slots. (2) In the data centre power BMS, if a circuit or PDU side is marked out, align that window with a loss of `grid-redundant` — Splunk is allowed to be ahead of a ticket if the BMS lags, but the pair should not contradict for hours. (3) If the inventory count disagrees, run `| search index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" classId=equipmentPsu <chassis_filter> | table _time, dn, operState` and compare the latest row per `dn` to the GUI; a mismatch is usually a poll-only gap or a mis-scoped index. (4) If `psuRedundancy` disagrees, pull the `equipmentChassisStats` object in the XML API browser for the same `dn` and compare attribute names. (5) If everything matches UCSM and you still do not like the `redundancy_delta` string, **do not** lower hardware thresholds here first — **edit the lookup row** or the `case()` order to match the signed-in architecture standard for your `n+n` hand-offs.

**Step 4 — Operationalize — dashboard, alert, RACI, calendars**
(1) Place the result table in **row 2** of the same dashboard tab you already use for UC-19.1.6 so operators see kW/thermal and redundancy posture in one place; the row-1 19.1.6 table stays the trend board, row-2 is the discrete posture board. (2) **RACI** (example): the server platform team (UCS) owns the alert configuration and the CSV; the data centre operations team is consulted for dual-feed, breaker, and rack PDU truth; the network team is informed only if the event correlates to fabric or IOM maintenance. (3) Set the saved search to run every 5 minutes with `dispatch.earliest_time = -35m` and `dispatch.latest_time = now` to overlap inventory lag only slightly, not hours. (4) On PagerDuty, route `severity_bucket=critical` to the phone, `warning` to Slack or email, and wire a **change-calendar** `suppress` from ServiceNow to a KV store or a small lookup updated nightly for PSU RMA, grid work, and firmware waves. (5) In test domains, add `| search chassis_dn!=*test*` so synthetic chassis never open bridge calls.

**Step 5 — Tuning and troubleshooting (10 practice bullets)**
• Mismatched nameplate part numbers between PSUs: inventory may list both as present but one may be derated; trust UCSM’s `psuRedundancy` after two polls, not a spreadsheet row alone. • X9508: PSU bay layout, IFM, and 6+ PSU support mean you must re-tune the lookup `psu_expected` from the factory BOM, not from a 5108 RMA. • Interaction with `adminPowerCappingPolicy`: aggressive capping can look like a “safe” state while redundancy is structurally lost; the MO hint field `ag_hint` in the `spl` is a breadcrumb, not a verdict — pair with 19.1.6 kW. • A chassis that shows `intact` in this UC can still be thermally bad; never retire 19.1.6. • Two inventory-only polls in a row with zero psu events for a full chassis: check whether the input class list dropped `equipmentPsu`. • A stats-only outage with good inventory: check whether the statistics input lost HTTPS trust after a UCSM cert rotation. • `chassis_model` with embedded spaces: trim in SPL if your props add whitespace. • Very large domains: consider a `by org` or `by site` `lookup` to split the alert into per-team queues. • After a UCSM restore from backup, MO order can re-ingest: expect a 15m spike in `unknown_admin_goal` while models repopulate. • Intersight-primary estates per exclusions: if you must keep this search for legacy clusters only, add `| search ucsm_node=*` on a `host` you tag, so you do not mix domains that no longer use UCSM. • RMA with only one new PSU in the spares cage and one old left installed: the numbers look `intact` but the wattage headroom is wrong — 19.1.6’s `equipmentPsuStats` can still be used as a secondary panel for watts, not a duplicate posture rule.
grandmaExplanation: We tell you when a blade chassis is down to one healthy power path even though the servers are still on—before the next fault turns the whole chassis dark. That is a different job than tracking how hot the air is, which a neighbour use case does with graphs.
hardware: Cisco blade chassis: UCSB 5108 (with IOM 2204, 2208, 2304, 2408, or 9108 IFM); UCS X9508 and X-chassis with IFM and multi-PSU layouts; B200/B400/B480 families living in those chassis. Pair with 6300/6400/6500 Fabric Interconnects that front the same UCSM domain; rack-only C-series in pure CIMC mode is not the primary scope.
